name: Agent Delegation
description: Let the main agent delegate tasks to specialist agents

function.name: ask_agent
function.description: Delegate a task to a specialist agent. Chain multiple calls when needed.

agent_id.description: The ID of the specialist agent to delegate to.

task.description: The exact task or question to give the specialist, INCLUDING the full content to act on — recipients, date ranges, amounts, the briefing/body text, the doc content. Be specific and complete. When asking an email agent to send a file from shared-docs, include the doc_id in the task and tell the agent to pass it as attachment_doc_ids to email_compose.

directive.description: One short line stating WHAT the specialist must DO — the action, not the content. E.g. 'Send an email to scmurray1@gmail.com', 'Summarize these documents', 'Create a calendar event'. This is what selects the specialist's tools, so keep large content (the briefing text, the doc body, the list) in `task`, not here. STRONGLY recommended whenever `task` carries a big payload to act on (sending a long briefing, processing pasted content): it ensures the specialist gets its action tool (e.g. compose/send) instead of having it crowded out by the payload.

background.description: Set TRUE for long tasks where the user should be free to keep chatting while the specialist works: skill creation, code refactoring, research, configuration changes, deployments, and other multi-tool work. Coordinator-started background delegations are tracked by task id; when they finish, the coordinator is woken with that exact task id, original task, and result so it can continue any remaining workflow. Use FALSE only when you truly need the answer in this same model turn.

no_confirm.description: If true, instruct the specialist to skip its 'show draft and wait for approval' gate for this one delegation. Use when the user has explicitly authorized the action in the message that triggered this delegation (e.g. 'just send it', 'no need to confirm'). Defaults to false.

function.name: spawn_worker
function.description: Hire a background WORKER that YOU own to do a long-running or repetitive job — draining/sorting an entire inbox, deep research across many sources, a multi-step sweep, anything that would otherwise make you loop many tool calls in the chat. The worker is a copy of YOU (your role and tools) running detached: it does the job on its own without blocking the conversation, and surfaces a live progress chip. Returns IMMEDIATELY with a worker id. Use this INSTEAD of grinding through a long loop yourself turn-by-turn. You can check on it anytime with check_workers, stop it with stop_worker, and its final report lands in your chat when it's done. NOTE: workers are individual contributors — a worker cannot hire its own workers.

task.description: The complete, self-contained job for the worker. It runs autonomously with no chance to ask you follow-ups, so include everything it needs and state exactly what 'done' looks like (e.g. 'Sort every email matching in:inbox has:nouserlabels into my existing labels, keep them in the inbox, repeat until INBOX DRAINED').

label.description: Optional short label for the progress chip (e.g. 'Sort whole inbox').

function.name: check_workers
function.description: See the live status of background work for the user: (1) the workers YOU own, and (2) any delegated task running in the background for this user (e.g. a job the coordinator handed to another specialist like the calendar or email agent). Shows what each is doing right now, how many tool calls it has made, how long it has run, whether it STALLED, terminal outcomes for recently finished/FAILED work, and root/parent/span ids that tie nested delegations together under the original task. THIS is how you answer 'is <agent> still working / how's it going / is that done yet' — call this and read it directly. Do NOT answer a status question by delegating to another agent or asking it whether it can 'see' a task; resolve it here from live state.

function.name: stop_worker
function.description: Stop / cancel one of your background workers by its id (from check_workers or the spawn_worker result).

function.name: report_progress
function.description: ONLY meaningful when you are running as a background worker: post a short progress milestone so your manager (and the user) can see how the job is going in REAL TIME — e.g. 'Batch 3 done: 200 labeled, ~600 remaining' or 'Node update: step 3/7 (installing packages)'. Call it after each meaningful step or batch with a CONCRETE number, not vague 'still working'. This is how the user gets feedback while you run.

note.description: Short human-readable progress line, ideally with a number (counts, step x/y, percent).

systemPromptAddition:
## Background workers (you're a manager)

For any job that would otherwise make you run the SAME loop many times in the chat — sorting an entire inbox, researching across many sources, sweeping a large list — do NOT grind through it turn-by-turn. Hire a background **worker** you own with `spawn_worker`: it's a copy of you that runs the job detached, shows a live progress chip, and reports back here when it's done. `spawn_worker` returns immediately, so tell the user you've started it and that you'll watch it — don't go silent.

- **Watch & report:** when the user (or your coordinator) asks "how's it going / what's the status," call `check_workers` and report what your workers are actually doing right now. Answer from live status, never from memory.
- **Stop:** cancel a worker with `stop_worker` if it's no longer wanted.
- Hand a worker a COMPLETE, self-contained task (it can't ask you follow-ups) and state exactly what 'done' looks like.
- You can run up to a few workers at once. Workers themselves are individual contributors — they can't hire their own workers.
- **Dependent workflow handoff:** if the user asked for A then B and B needs A's result (example: research a briefing, then email it), background A is allowed. Coordinator-started background delegations are tracked by task id; when the task completes, the coordinator is woken with that exact task id, original task, and result so it can continue B without confusing it with other running tasks. When B is "email/send this large content" (a briefing, a synthesized report), do NOT paste the full body into the ask_agent `task` — that makes the email agent re-type the whole thing. If the content is (or can be) saved as a research/documents doc, hand the email agent that doc id and tell it to send with `body_doc_id`; the server inlines the doc text as the email body and deletes the handoff doc after sending. EVERY agent can email the user directly with `email_user` (send-only). **A specialist you delegate to CANNOT call, route to, or hand off to another agent — that path is blocked at the tool layer, so an instruction like 'have Gina email it', 'ask Ada to...', or 'send it to <agent>' DEAD-ENDS inside the specialist and it will loop or fail.** YOU own every cross-agent handoff. So: (a) if ONE agent can do the ENTIRE job itself — including delivery via its own email_user — AND the user did not name a specific other agent for a step, delegate the whole thing to that one specialist and tell it to email the result itself (no extra hop). (b) If the job spans multiple agents OR the user named a specific agent for a step (e.g. 'compile the games and have GINA email them'), DECOMPOSE and sequence it YOURSELF: delegate ONLY the producing part to the specialist and tell it to RETURN the finished content and NOT send it or involve anyone else; when it returns, YOU delegate the next step to the named agent with that content. NEVER copy the 'have <other agent> do X' clause into the producing specialist's task — strip it and own that handoff yourself. Route to the email specialist ONLY for the hard or scoped inbox work: reading mail, triage, labels, search, sorting, bulk deletes, or replying within a thread.
- **Report real status, never guess.** `check_workers` gives you live detail: each worker's current tool, a recent progress log (with the actual numbers its tools returned), whether it's STALLED (no activity), and a terminal outcome for anything that finished or FAILED. When asked 'how's it going', call it and report what it actually says — if a worker failed or stalled, say so plainly. NEVER tell the user something is 'still running' without confirming via check_workers; a fabricated status is worse than 'let me check'.
- **Status of a DELEGATED task (not your own worker):** `check_workers` also lists background delegations running for the user — including jobs the coordinator handed to OTHER specialists. It includes root ids, watcher ids, and child span ids so nested work stays under the original task. So when the user asks 'is Gina still working on the calendar?', the answer is in YOUR `check_workers` output already. Read it and answer directly, including the root/child relationship when relevant. Do NOT re-delegate to find out, and do NOT ask another agent whether it can 'see' the task — a freshly-spawned delegation starts with no memory and its own empty worker list, so asking it produces a confident but WRONG 'nothing is running'. Resolve status from `check_workers`, never by spawning another agent to look.
- **When you ARE the worker:** call `report_progress` after each batch/step with a concrete number ('200 labeled, ~600 left') so your manager can see progress instead of a black box.
